blocks.html:
MCP is an open standard for plugging AI applications into external systems — think of it as USB for AI integrations. A company builds one MCP server for its ticketing system, and every MCP-capable client (Claude Code, desktop apps, custom agents) can use it. That reuse is the point: if five AI tools need the same data, build the server once. If only one app needs one bespoke workflow, a custom in-app tool is simpler.

Servers expose three kinds of things, distinguished by who controls them: tools (model-controlled actions — search, update, send), resources (application-controlled context — schemas, catalogs, docs), and prompts (user-invoked reusable workflows). The classic design question — "resource or tool?" — has a clean rule: stable reference material the agent consults before acting is a resource; dynamic computation at the moment of use is a tool. They're complements: resources say what's true, tools change it.

One thing MCP does NOT do: solve auth, rate limiting, retries, or caching. It's a protocol, not a middleware platform.

Your data-warehouse server exposes get_schemas() and run_query(sql), and every session burns its first turn fetching schemas. Fix: expose the schemas as a resource the application provides as context, keep run_query as a tool. And when the agent ignores your specialized dependency-analysis tool in favor of generic shell commands, the fix is a better description — when to prefer it, inputs/outputs, its unique capabilities — not removing the generic tools the agent legitimately needs.

Exam-grade specifics:

  • Annotations are not security. readOnlyHint, destructiveHint, etc. are unverified server self-reports — use them to pick which confirmation UI to show, never to skip a required security check.
  • Scaling the surface: tool search / progressive availability loads tool definitions on demand; list_changed notifications let servers announce new capabilities mid-session. Write descriptions that read well in isolation.
  • Claude Code scopes: project (.mcp.json, checked in, team-shared), local (~/.claude.json keyed to the project — personal credentials, experiments), user (~/.claude.json global — personal tools everywhere). Same name at multiple scopes → one connection, precedence local > project > user, whole entry wins, no field merging. Personal credentials never belong in project scope.
  • MCP prompts surface as slash commands (mcp__server__prompt).

items: Resource = stable reference consulted before acting; tool = dynamic computation. Expose both.
MCP's value is reuse across clients; it solves integration shape, not auth/retries/caching.
Agent ignoring your tool? Fix the description, don't delete the generic tools.
Annotations are untrusted hints — UI affordances yes, skipped security checks never.
Scopes: project is shared via the repo; local/user stay on your machine; local > project > user.
